sections.heading: Overview
sections.paragraph: The pharmaceutical landscape is undergoing a significant transformation driven by the increasing structural complexity of modern small molecules, particularly targeted protein degraders and covalent agents. WuXi AppTec highlights that classical workflows are insufficient for these advanced modalities, which demand integrated approaches connecting discovery, development, and manufacturing under a unified quality system. Targeted protein degraders function by converting transient binding events into the catalytic removal of target proteins, introducing unique challenges such as ternary complex formation and linker topology optimization. To address translational success rates, the industry is shifting from compound-centric processes to outcome-oriented disciplines that leverage technologies like DNA-encoded libraries, fragment-based screening, and flow chemistry. WuXi AppTec emphasizes that early process development is critical for degraders to mitigate risks associated with synthetic complexity and analytical performance during scale-up. By maintaining consistent certifications and audit history across global operations, contract research, development, and manufacturing organizations can prevent variability that derails timelines. This integrated CRDMO model allows partners to compress development timelines while preserving continuity from the first gram to commercial batch. Consequently, the focus is evolving from target druggability to target engagement strategy, ensuring that scientific insight and development capabilities are connected across the entire discovery continuum to support the clinical advancement of complex small molecule therapeutics.

points.text: WuXi AppTec — Targeted Protein Degraders — General — Integrated CRDMO support for clinical advancement
WuXi AppTec: Providing integrated end-to-end CRDMO services to support complex small molecule discovery and manufacturing.
Targeted protein degraders convert transient binding events into catalytic removal of target proteins, requiring optimization of ternary complex formation and linker topology.
